title: From Idea to Shelf
subtitle: ChocoNation wants a mango flavour by summer. Put the journey in order.

gameplay.scenario: It's January. ChocoNation wants to launch a mango-chocolate bar before summer. Between the idea in your head and a customer picking it off a kirana shelf lies a pipeline — and doing the steps out of order wastes money and time. Arrange the journey.

steps.label: Talk to 20 customers about the mango idea
steps.explain: Ideas are cheap; wrong ideas are expensive. Founders test demand BEFORE spending on development — if students and shopkeepers shrug at 'mango chocolate', you just saved months and lakhs.

steps.label: Develop small test batches in the kitchen and refine the recipe
steps.explain: Development comes after you know people want it. Small batches let you fail cheaply — a bad recipe at 2kg costs pocket money; at 2 tonnes it costs the company.

steps.label: Calculate cost per bar and set the selling price
steps.explain: Before mass production, you must know the unit economics: if mango pulp pushes cost to ₹24 and customers won't pay above ₹35, your margin per bar is ₹11 — decide NOW whether that works, not after making 50,000 bars.

steps.label: Order ingredients in bulk and run full production
steps.explain: Production scales up only once the recipe is locked and the maths works. Bulk ordering cuts your ingredient cost — but it also locks in your money, which is why it sits this late.

steps.label: Arrange cold-chain vans and deliver stock to stores
steps.explain: Logistics: chocolate in Indian summer needs cool transport, or your product melts before it earns. Distribution is planned around production output — you can't deliver what isn't made.

steps.label: Launch in stores with posters and a tasting weekend
steps.explain: The launch goes last: marketing works best when the product is actually ON the shelf. Promoting a bar people can't find burns money and goodwill.
